To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any referral pathways in place for patients, from each Health and Social Care Trust, to access specialist endometriosis services, including any multidisciplinary team services; and (ii) any guidance that has been issued to General Practitioners and Emergency Departments in relation to these referral pathways.
answer_text: All Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts currently provide care for women with endometriosis, with referrals managed under the care of a consultant gynaecologist. Gynaecology services are delivered through multidisciplinary teams to ensure holistic, responsive and appropriate care throughout the patient journey. Each member of the team whether a consultant, nurse or allied health professional acts as a specialist within their own discipline.A Northern Ireland Endometriosis Pathway has been agreed and was developed in partnership with stakeholders across both primary and secondary care. This pathway has been shared with Trust Gynaecology Units and the Gynaecology Provider Collaborative. It sets out the patient journey from initial presentation in primary care, through enhanced primary care services and local endometriosis provision, and onwards to the developing Regional Endometriosis Centres, as well as covering posttreatment followup.The diagnostic pathway includes assessment of symptoms, investigations to determine severity and disease progression, and the planning of appropriate next steps. Management options range from ongoing monitoring to hormonal therapies and/or surgical intervention, with consideration of fertility needs throughout.My Department is working closely with the Gynaecology Provider Collaborative to invest across all five Trusts. This investment aims to enhance diagnostic capacity, increase the number of laparoscopy sessions, and expand clinical appointment availability. In addition, work is progressing to develop two Regional Endometriosis Centres within the Western and Belfast Trusts. These centres will provide complex surgical care for women who require specialist intervention, supported by multidisciplinary teams including colorectal and urological surgeons.The model being developed aligns with the national Endometriosis Pathway; however, available funding will not allow for full implementation of all pathway components at this stage. As a result, development will proceed on a phased basis to ensure services can be expanded safely and sustainably.

To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department (i) is monitoring the development of new urine-based diagnostic tests for conditions such as breast cancer, endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome; and (ii) has had any engagement with researchers on their potential future use within the health service.
answer_text: These diagnostic tests for conditions such as breast cancer, endometriosis and polycystic ovary syndrome are currently at a very early stage of development. As the Department is required to operate in accordance with NICE guidance, any use of such tests for diagnostic purposes would require specific approval by NICE.Should NICE approval be granted in due course, officials would consider the commissioning implications, including appropriate monitoring and evaluation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans to improve and expand endometriosis services, including (i) access to diagnosis; (ii) specialist treatment; and (iii) the reduction of waiting times for both urgent and routine patients.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that we have the right model of care, in the right place, at the right time. In this context, we have secured and delivered a Getting It Right First Time report on gynaecology and are progressing the implementation of its recommendations.A Regional Gynaecology Forum has been established to take this work forward, with an Endometriosis Task and Finish Group operating as part of this. This regional group is developing services along the patient pathway, from initial presentation through to diagnostic assessment and onto treatment either locally or in a tertiary centre.All HSC Trusts provide care for endometriosis, with women referred under the care of a consultant gynaecologist. Gynaecology services are delivered through multidisciplinary teams to ensure holistic, responsive and appropriate care throughout the patient journey. Each team member, whether a consultant, nurse, or allied health professional functions as a specialist within their discipline.The diagnostic pathway includes symptom assessment, investigations to determine severity and progression, and planning appropriate next steps. Management options range from ongoing monitoring to hormonal therapies and/or surgical intervention, with consideration of fertility needs throughout.The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) is working with the Gynaecology Collaborative to invest in all five Trusts, enhancing capacity for diagnostics, additional laparoscopy sessions and clinical appointments. Work is also underway to develop two regional Endometriosis Centres within the Western and Belfast Trusts. These centres will provide complex surgical care for women who require it, supported by multidisciplinary teams of surgeons, including colorectal and urological consultants.The development of endometriosis services remains one of my key priorities for improvements in women’s health and my officials are expecting the submission of the relevant business cases by March 2026. However, there is insufficient funding to ensure that all of the capacity required for endometriosis services will be secured with the funding currently available and the expansion of this service will need to be phased as further funding is identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given recent reports that testing menstrual blood for HPV could offer a less invasive and potentially more acceptable alternative to traditional cervical cancer screening, improving uptake among people who currently avoid smear tests, whether his Department is assessing this new approach.
answer_text: Screening policy across the UK is informed by the recommendations of the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent committee which advises the four UK health Departments on screening matters.  The UK NSC regularly reviews the scientific evidence in relation to existing recommendations which includes proactive searching of sources for new developments in research, international policy making and emerging technologies. These evidence reviews inform the UK NSC’s recommendations.In June 2025 the UKNSC reviewed the evidence on human papilloma virus (HPV) testing using a self-collected vaginal sample for cervical cancer screening (self-sampling) and recommended that cervical screening providers should be allowed to offer a self-sampling test option to women who never or rarely attend their routine cervical screening appointments.  This UKNSC recommendation will be considered by the Northern Ireland Screening Committee before officials put forward their policy advice for my consideration.

To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on (i) the implementation of the Women’s Health Action Plan, including (ii) any actions that have been completed to date; (iii) what remains outstanding; and (iv) the planned timeline for the next phases of delivery.
answer_text: I plan to publish the Women's Health Action Plan following completion of the public listening exercise over the coming months. My Department has helped to fund this important project by Derry Well Women and Queen’s University Belfast who have now completed a women’s health survey and focus groups with harder to reach women. There will be a further round of engagement with stakeholders during February to share the findings and to identify priorities and recommendations. The Plan when published will describe the wide range of policy and service developments supporting women's health, what has been achieved to date and what is achievable in the years ahead within current resources. In addition to delivering against the set of actions, the Plan aims to encourage positive dialogue around women’s health and wellbeing within our health and social care system and in our society. It is therefore important that delivery is coordinated in partnership with patient and public representatives who have been instrumental in raising the profile of women's health in Northern Ireland and ensuring that women’s voices and experiences are heard and reflected in the Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether he is (i) aware of the recent guidance from the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence recommending new more personalised blood test thresholds to help general practitioners detect ovarian cancer earlier; and (ii) considering reviewing or updating the ovarian cancer detection protocols to incorporate similar age-based screening thresholds to improve early diagnosis and health outcomes for women.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Clinical Guidelines are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions.My Department has endorsed the relevant NICE guidance on the recognition and referral of suspected cancer (NG12), however I am aware that on 5 January 2026, NICE launched a public consultation on a draft update to NG12, which proposes new age-based blood test thresholds to guide General Practitioner (GP) referral for further ovarian cancer investigation, in a bid to improve early diagnosis. The NICE public consultation on the draft recommendations ran until 2 February 2026. Final guidance is expected on 25 March 2026.When the final guidance is issued by NICE, my Department will consider it under the existing processes for NICE Clinical Guidelines. Any potential review or updating of the ovarian cancer detection protocols will be informed by best practice guidance including any revised NICE guidance on suspected cancer.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline any plans it has to replace the security hut at the visitors’ lower car park.

To ask the Minister of Health, given that the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence approved ribociclib (Kisqali) for early breast cancer on 6 August 2025, to detail (i) whether the Strategic Planning and Performance Group, through the regional Managed Entry and formulary process, approved ribociclib and requested all Health and Social Care Trusts (HSCT) prescribed it if available; (ii) whether his Department has approved the additional resources requested by HSCTs; (iii) whether a policy decision been taken to delay treatment until all HSCTs are ready to commence simultaneously; (
answer_text: The Department of Health (NI) has a formal link with NICE under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability in Northern Ireland. Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within HSC organisations. In practice this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also routinely available in Northern Ireland.		In the guidance published by NICE on 6th August 2025 (TA1086), Ribociclib (Kisqali®) was recommended with an aromatase inhibitor as an option for the adjuvant treatment of hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative, early breast cancer at high risk of recurrence. This guidance has been formally endorsed by the Department and was approved for use within the HSC under the Managed Entry of New Medicines process on 18th September 2025. The drug is available for use under interim commissioning (cost-per-case) arrangements. Details of the HSC Managed Entry decision are available at https://niformulary.hscni.net/managed-entry/managed-entry-decisions/Officers within my Strategic Planning and Performance Group have been working with colleagues from HSC Trusts to confirm the number of eligible patients and to agree a proposed service model to deliver this drug therapy. In the discussions to consider the resource implementation needs for TA1086 (Ribociclib), the Trusts have advised that given the numbers of expected patients and existing system capacity, there is a need to prospectively agree the additional staffing and testing infrastructure.  This will require an agreed approach to secure and train the additional staffing. It is proposed that an interim service model be established on a non-recurrent basis before transitioning to the recurrent model when the additional staffing required are available.SPPG officers during discussions with the Trusts have been advised that other UK Nations are facing similar challenges when considering similar models of implementation for this particular NICE TA.SPPG officers continue to work at pace to secure agreement on the implementation of arrangements of an interim model for NI. While this model is being put in place eligible patients have access to the medicine under interim commissioning (cost-per-case) arrangements where existing capacity allows. I hope that this information provides assurance of the significant efforts being made to allow introduction of Ribociclib on a planned and effective basis, and at the earliest possible opportunity for eligible patients in Northern Ireland.

To ask the Minister of Education to outline the level of cooperation that exists between his Department and the Department of Health in relation to special educational needs provision.
answer_text: In line with the duty to co-operate set out in the Children’s Service Co-operation (Northern Ireland) Act 2015, my officials continue to work closely with their counterparts in the Department of Health (DoH), the Education Authority (EA), the Public Health Agency (PHA) and the local Health Trusts across the interdependent systems to support children with SEN across education to ensure improved outcomes for children with special educational needs (SEN) and their families.There was significant co-operation between my Department and the Department of Health throughout the End-to-End Review of SEN which concluded in October 2024, with Health represented at all levels of the governance structures.  That co-operation has continued in the development and implementation of the SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Plan, which recognises the need to collaborate with the Department of Health as a delivery partner.  Officials from across different parts of the Department of Health including learning disability and autism policy, nursing and Allied Health Professionals are represented across the governance structures and are working closely with my officials in the implementation of the funded actions within the Delivery Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health,in relation to the ten independent children’s residential facilities currently operating, to detail (i) the number that are (a) run by charities or not-for-profit organisations; and (b) operated on a private or for-profit basis; (ii) how many of the children currently placed in privately owned or for-profit residential care homes have additional needs; (iii) what comparison has been made between the costs of placements in for-profit facilities and those provided directly by the public sector; and (iv) whether he has any plans to limit or prevent further growth of
answer_text: While currently it is possible for a for-profit operation to register as a provider of a children’s home in Northern Ireland, only a small number have done so.  At 28 October 2025, there were 59 children’s residential homes in Northern Ireland, of which 49 homes were operated by statutory sector and 10 by independent sector providers.  The independent sector providers include 4 providers (of 7 homes) operating on a not-for-profit and 2 providers (of 3 homes) on a for-profit basis.  In addition, at 30 June 2025 statutory homes provided 275 places at an average of six places per home; independent homes provided 26 places at an average of three per home.  The 3 homes operated by for-profit providers are registered with RQIA to provide accommodation to a maximum of 7 children in total.Due to the risk of children being identified it would not be appropriate to provide statistics on the needs of individual children within those homes operated by for-profit providers.  However, of the 10 independent children’s homes in Northern Ireland, care is provided to children in the categories of both emotional and behavioural difficulties and those with learning difficulties.The cost of a placement will be influenced and mainly determined by the needs of individual children, and therefore comparisons cannot necessarily be drawn on the costs of placements between statutory and independent provision.  It is the responsibility of Health and Social Care Trusts in securing any placement provision, to ensure these are value for money.An Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services in Northern Ireland was published in June 2023. That Review made a recommendation not to allow the privatisation of the care of children. In its response to the Review, the Department partially accepted that recommendation, recognising the need to keep private sector provision of children’s social care to a minimum, but also acknowledging the small number of for-profit providers currently operating here and the increasing pressure on services. My Department has established a programme of reform which is leading on the implementation of a number of the Review’s recommendations.  Enhancing capacity and capability of statutory residential care provision is a specific focus of the Programme, in particular through ongoing work to develop a regional strategy for children’s residential care. As shown in the table below, the number of children in care in Northern Ireland has increased year on year.  My Department will continue to strive to ensure that children in care receive the best package of care available to them.31 March 20203,38331 March 20213,53031 March 20223,62431 March 20233,80131 March 20243,99931 March 20254,188My Department publishes Official Statistics on the number of children in care in the annual report ‘Children’s Social Care Statistics for Northern Ireland’ (Children's social care statistics for Northern Ireland 2024/25 | Department of Health).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail whether (i) any assessment been made of the impact of the temporary pause on the recruitment of agency social work and social care staff, particularly in relation to staffing pressures, service delivery, and the continuity of support for vulnerable individuals and families (ii) any measures have been put in place to ensure the integrity of recruitment processes for agency health and social care staff following the discovery of fake references within agency-supplied workers; and (iii) all Health and Social Care Trusts have now resumed the recruitment of a
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts ceased using agency social workers in 2023.  As a result, 160 former recruitment agency social workers were recruited into HSC posts. Since September 2023 to date, all of the five Trusts have confirmed to the Department that there have been no recruitment agency social workers employed in HSC services. Recruitment processes have been developed to facilitate newly qualified social workers moving quickly into vacant posts, with on average 220 joining the HSC workforce annually since agency cessation.  The use of agency social care staff is ongoing; however actions were taken after it was discovered that some individuals had been employed by agencies who had supplied fraudulent references during their recruitment process.  This concern related to both social care and health care staff.  A regional oversight group was established to facilitate the sharing of intelligence and identification of any actions relating to the discovery of fraudulent references.  The group membership includes senior representatives from Counter Fraud and Probity Service (CFPS), Business Services Organisation (BSO), Health and Social Care Trusts, Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA), Northern Ireland Social Care Council (NISCC), Procurement and Logistics Service (PALS), and Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI).   The regional group is responsible for managing all the issues relating to fraudulent references of health care staff as well as social care staff.   All recruitment agencies have been asked to review the references of staff that were provided as part of their employment application.  Any individuals who have been discovered with fraudulent references have been removed from all Trust placements. Health and Social Care Trusts continue to recruit agency social care staff with due regard to the issues relating to fraudulent references.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline the actions his Department is taking to ensure that patients requiring regular cancer treatment are able to access ambulance transport between different Health and Social Trust areas.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any immediate action which will be taken in relation to patients being denied ambulance transport for regular cancer treatment due to Health and Social Care Trust boundaries.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) provides ambulance transport for patients whose clinical needs mean they cannot reasonably make their own way to or from hospital or other health and social care facilities. The framework for this provision is set out in my department’s ‘Transport Strategy for Health and Social Care Services in Northern Ireland’.Non-emergency ambulance transport is provided on the basis of medical need, assessed against eligibility criteria which takes account of the patient’s mobility, clinical condition and a potential necessity for professional assistance during the journey. Where a patient is assessed as eligible, transport is arranged by NIAS through co-ordination with Health and Social Care Trusts. My department has confirmed that NIAS does not operate a policy of denying transport based on Trust boundaries. NIAS routinely provides transport for cancer patients on a regional basis.If there are specific instances where transport has been unavailable, I would encourage these to be brought to our attention. My department would welcome the opportunity to review any such cases to ensure appropriate support is being provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he is considering further reducing or removing the current 52-week waiting list requirement for patients to qualify for reimbursement under the Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme.
answer_text: The Waiting List Reimbursement Scheme was initiated on a phased approach. The first phase granted reimbursements for treatment in the Republic of Ireland (Rol). Phase Two, launched 15 September 2025, extends access to the wider European Union.I have also decided to reduce the qualifying period from two years to one year, and by widening access to the EU, I am giving more patients more options to get the treatment they need.This scheme is about listening to patients, being flexible, and taking practical action to ease pressure on waiting lists. This decision is a data informed decision supplemented by active listening across the service and delivering on my commitment to best maximise this important initiative for patient benefit and waiting list reduction. At this time, we have no further plan to adjust the Scheme parameters and will continue to monitor its uptake.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any progress has been made on plans to allow early access to pensions for those who have a terminal illness diagnosis.

To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the rationale for not reporting review waiting times for people with multiple sclerosis separately from wider neurology service data; (ii) why referrals from neurologists to other disciplines such as mental health services, neuro-rehabilitation, and physiotherapy are not routinely reported; and (iii) whether consideration will be given to including this information in future reporting, given its potential value for workforce planning, future-proofing services, identifying investment needs and highlighting risk factors for people with multiple sclero
answer_text: As outlined in my previous response on this area, services for people with multiple sclerosis (MS) are provided as part of wider Neurology services in each Trust area.The information on review waiting times for people with a diagnosis of MS and indeed for other subspecialist areas within this specialty has historically been reported in the monitoring information provided for the wider neurology service and has not therefore been identified separately.The information on referrals for those under the care of a Neurologist referred to other disciplines including mental health services, neuro rehabilitation and physio are internal referrals within a Trust setting and have not been subject to reporting as part of performance management arrangements. As you will be aware, the HSC is in the process of implementing a new digital care record across the region with the Encompass system now available in each Trust area. The process is underway to ensure system optimisation including consideration of the reporting that will be available from the system. In is intended that the new digital health record system will allow an improved level of reporting which as you have indicated has the potential to provide data that will inform service needs for those across a range of chronic long-term conditions.This is a welcome development and important in the context of the current review waiting times for neurology and wider support services which as previously acknowledged are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what steps his Department has taken to ensure that Operation Begrain is proceeding as a robust and independent inquiry; (ii) to outline why no senior officials from the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust or his Department have to date been questioned in connection with the withholding of records, patient harm, or governance failings arising from the neurology recall; and (iii) to detail what actions his Department is taking to promote transparency and justice for affected patients and families.
answer_text: The Police Service of Northern Ireland’s (PSNI) Operation Begrain is a live police investigation into the clinical practices of the former consultant neurologist Michael Watt.  The PSNI has operational independence in its leadership of this inquiry. The Department of Health does not comment on live police investigations or operational policing matters.In common with all parts of the Health and Social Care system, my Department will cooperate fully with the PSNI as it progresses its investigations. On 11 April 2025 an Information Sharing Agreement was signed between the Department of Health and PSNI to this end. The PSNI’s website does provide updates and points of contact in regard to Operation Begrain – the following link may be helpful to you  -  www.psni.police.uk/latest-news/detectives-police-services-major-investigation-team-issue-update-operation-begrain

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current average waiting time for patients diagnosed with multiple sclerosis for (i) review appointments; (ii) mental health services; (iii) neuro rehabilitation; and (iv) physiotherapy, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Services for people with multiple sclerosis (MS) are provided as part of wider Neurology services in each Trust area. There has been detailed engagement with the clinical teams in each Trust over the past number of years with a view to improving the care and treatment available for people with this condition. As part of this engagement, a Service Specification was developed, consistent with NICE Clinical Guidelines for the service, which sets out the requirements for delivering a multi-disciplinary service consistently across the region. The priorities that were identified from the Service Specification were for all Trusts to have dedicated clinics for people with MS, for each Trust to have dedicated MS nursing staff and for the capacity to be available in each Trust to allow infusion delivered disease modifying treatments (DMTs) to be available locally.Investments were made over a number of years and each of these priority objectives have been achieved. Colleagues from DoH work closely with HSC Trusts each year to consider the requirements associated with the increasing number of people with MS who are on DMTs. Over £20m is invested across the region in supporting the provision of approved drug treatments to eligible patients. The information on review waiting times for people with a diagnosis of MS is included in the monitoring information provided for the wider neurology service and is not identified separately. The information on referrals for those under the care of a Neurologist referred to other disciplines including mental health services, neuro rehabilitation and physio is not routinely reported. I acknowledge that the current review waiting times for neurology and wider support services are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how the Regional Review of Neurology Services fits within the Health and Social Care Reset Plan.
answer_text: My Department’s Health and Social Care Reset Plan, published on 9 July 2025 sets out a specific commitment to take forward and commence implementation of the reform and configuration of a number of key services including neurology services. The recommendations proposed in the final report of my Department’s Regional Review of Neurology Services fully align with this commitment. The final report of the Review recommends significant growth in the neurology workforce and an improved skill mix to address capacity shortfalls, reduce waiting lists and ensure equity in access to services. In addition, the report proposes a range of recommendations to shift services towards person-centred and local community-based care. Consideration of new and innovative ways of working is also recommended to drive efficiencies and ensure the best use of current resources.Implementation of the recommendations proposed in the final report of the Review will require considerable additional investment at a time when my Department is facing significant in year budgetary pressures. It may be the case, therefore, that the pace of implementation will have to be adjusted in line with the funding available. I will however carefully consider funding to implement the Neurology Review in the context of future budget settlements and competing priorities.A public consultation on the final report of the Neurology Review ran from 7 May 2025 until 6 August 2025. Now that the consultation has closed my officials are currently reviewing the responses to the consultation which will inform a final implementation plan. I expect this work to be completed in the coming months.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current average waiting time from initial referral to diagnosis for multiple sclerosis, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Services for people with multiple sclerosis (MS) are provided as part of wider Neurology services in each Trust area. There has been detailed engagement with the clinical teams in each Trust over the past number of years with a view to improving the care and treatment available for people with this condition. As part of this engagement, a Service Specification was developed, consistent with NICE Clinical Guidelines for the service, which sets out the requirements for delivering a multi-disciplinary service consistently across the region. The priorities that were identified from the Service Specification were for all Trusts to have dedicated clinics for people with MS, for each Trust to have dedicated MS nursing staff and for the capacity to be available in each Trust to allow infusion delivered disease modifying treatments (DMTs) to be available locally.Investments were made over a number of years and each of these priority objectives have been achieved. Colleagues from DoH work closely with HSC Trusts each year to consider the requirements associated with the increasing number of people with MS who are on DMTs. Over £20m is invested across the region in supporting the provision of approved drug treatments to eligible patients. Work has also taken place with clinical teams in each Trust to develop guidelines for referral of people with suspected MS. These guidelines are available on the electronic referral system utilised by General Practice to refer into the hospital-based neurology teams. The information included as part of a referral allows the clinical teams undertaking the triage to consider patient need and allocate people to the relevant clinic. The information on waiting times for people referred with suspected MS is included in the monitoring information provided for the wider neurology service and is not identified separately. I am not, therefore, able to provide information on the average waiting time from initial referral to diagnosis for MS specifically.I acknowledge that the current waiting times for neurology and wider support services are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed to addressing this, subject to available funding.

Colleagues from DoH work closely with HSC Trusts each year to consider the requirements associated with the increasing number of people with MS who are on DMTs. Over £20m is invested across the region in supporting the provision of approved drug treatments to eligible patients. When I published the HSC Reset Plan in July 2025, the commitments in the plan were to Stabilise the HSC system,Drive forward our system Reform, and Increase and improve how we Deliver our servicesWhilst not specifically referred to in the plan, I acknowledge that the current new and review waiting times for neurology and wider support services are lengthy and growing for adults across Northern Ireland. This situation has arisen due to several factors, including funding that falls short of objectively assessed need and previous missed opportunities to reform health and social care delivery. I am committed as part of the process to reset services, to addressing this, subject to available funding.As part of a process to consider the wider pressures on neurology services, the Department commissioned a Regional Review of Neurology Services. The review was tasked with considering the optimum service configuration for neurology services for the next 10-15 years. The final report of the Neurology Review was published as part of a consultation exercise in May 2025.A series of consultation events were held over the summer months and the Review Team are now in the process of considering the input received from the consultation events and from responses received on the report before considering next steps. Progress on taking for the agreed priorities set out in the report will be subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether it intends to provide enhanced facilities in Parliament Buildings to promote a healthy lifestyle.
To ask the Minister of Health for a timeline for the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence approval for Givinostat for eligible patients with Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline the average time between National Institute for Health and Care Excellence approval of a treatment and its availability to patients through the NHS.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. In practice, this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also available in Northern Ireland.HSC Circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22 sets out the process for the endorsement, implementation, monitoring and assurance in Northern Ireland for NICE Technology Appraisals. The Departmental endorsement of NICE Technology Appraisals is expected to be complete within four weeks of the final publication by NICE. My Department will normally issue a Service Notification to HSC Trusts and other relevant providers and stakeholders, setting out the expectations for implementation within a further 15 weeks.However, the Process for the Managed Entry of New Medicines provides the Department with the discretion to commission treatments when NICE have published Final Draft Guidance (FDGs), near-final versions of Technology Appraisals, approximately 6 weeks prior to the final publication.This discretion may only be applied where supporting infrastructure is in place and the service has capacity. Requests for treatment within these arrangements are normally considered on a cost-per-case basis where individuals meet the necessary criteria specified by NICE.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he intends to make Givinostat available under the (i) Early Access to Medicines Scheme; and (ii) Early Access Programme.
answer_text: (i) The Early Access to Medicines Scheme is operated by the Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) and enables access to promising new medicines and treatments before they have obtained a marketing authorisation, where there is a clear unmet medical need.  Since givinostat was awarded conditional marketing authorisation by the MHRA on 24 December 2024, it is not eligible for the Early Access to Medicines Scheme. (ii) I refer the member to my response to AQW 27727/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timeline of the process for the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence to consider whether to approve access to Givinostat for eligible patients with Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
answer_text: Details on the timeline for the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) to consider givinostat as an appropriate treatment for Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy are available on the organisation’s website at Project information | Givinostat for treating Duchenne muscular dystrophy in people 6 years and over [ID6323] | Guidance | NICEhttps://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/indevelopment/gid-ta11373. The expected publication date for final guidance on the use of givinostat in eligible patients is 18 September 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any preparatory work his Department has carried out in advance of National Institute for Health and Care Excellence consideration as to whether to approve access to Givinostat for eligible patients with Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
answer_text: The Department’s process for the endorsement, implementation, monitoring and assurance of new National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) approved medicines is set out in Circular HSC (SQSD) 12/22, available on the Department of Health website.While the process does not require preparatory work by the Department of Health in advance of any decision by NICE, my officials have liaised with the Belfast HSC to understand the potential resource requirements for the provision of safe and sustainable patient access to givinostat, should NICE recommend its use.  In addition and following my recent meeting with the families of children with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy, I have asked that the Belfast Trust have plans in place to enable recruitment and implementation to proceed at pace in the event of a positive recommendation on givinostat by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) this Autumn.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the timeline for the introduction of the Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses redress scheme legislation.
answer_text: We met with victims and survivors from the Consultation Forum in advance of the introduction of the Inquiry (Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses) and Redress Scheme Bill on 16 June 2025. 	The Bill completed its Second Stage on 24 June 2025 and began its Committee Stage the following day.This Bill represents a significant milestone – it will help to support truth, acknowledgement and accountability for victims and survivors.  This legislation will establish an independent statutory inquiry with significant powers, and secondly, it will provide an initial redress payment to thousands of victims and survivors.This is a complex and sensitive piece of legislation, with many constraints and often differing views to balance in the decision-making process.  There has been constructive engagement with the Executive Office Committee, and we look forward to supporting them in their important scrutiny role. We appreciate the Member has been involved in this work, and we would be happy for our officials to provide a dedicated briefing on the legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the recent announcements that eligible patients with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy in England and Scotland will be able to access Givinostat via the Early Access Programme in some hospitals.  Can the Minister confirm if his Department will be making Givinostat available to eligible patients with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy here via the Early Access Programme.
answer_text: I am aware that several health trusts in other parts of the UK have now agreed to participate in the manufacturer’s Early Access Programme (EAP) for givinostat.  Compassionate use schemes such as EAPs permit health providers to make new licensed treatments available to patients free of charge while they are being assessed for clinical and cost-effectiveness by the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE), however they do not provide any additional resources which are often required to safely administer the drug and monitor patients.Whilst it is an operational decision for trusts to participate in an EAP and identify the additional resources required prior to a recommendation by NICE, all trusts are constrained by the severe budgetary pressures currently facing the Health and Social Care (HSC) system and the urgent need to reduce costs.  It is my understanding that in light of this it has not been possible to identify the additional funding that would be required to support the EAP for givinostat. My officials have been in touch with the Belfast HSC Trust following our recent meeting with the families of children with Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy, to request that plans are put in place to enable recruitment and implementation to proceed at pace in the event of a positive recommendation on givinostat by NICE this Autumn.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice when the Sentencing Bill will be introduced to the Assembly.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on her Department’s recent Inclusive Transport and Travel conference.
answer_text: The Inclusive Transport and Travel conference was a significant and positive event organised by my department. It brought together various stakeholders, including industry experts, and representatives from disability advocacy groups and, most importantly, people with lived experience, to discuss and promote inclusive transport and accessible places solutions.During the conference, several key topics were covered, including the implementation of new accessibility standards, advancements in assistive technologies, and strategies for improving public awareness about inclusive transport. It is clear that collaboration and innovation are key factors in achieving these goals and my department is listening and looking to the future for new and innovative solutions. We are under no illusions about the scale of the challenge, but we will continue to take positive action, fostering meaningful discussions and paving the way for future initiatives to enhance the inclusivity of transport systems and accessible spaces.Feedback from attendees has been overwhelmingly positive and responses stressed the importance of inclusive design and having those most affected by our Policy choices at the table from the start to finish of the process. In preparation for the Conference, the DfI Senior team participated in Disability Equality Training. The practical component of the training was conducted by individuals with disabilities who guided senior DfI officials on a journey through Belfast. This allowed the officials to experience firsthand the daily challenges faced by people with disabilities when navigating our road and public transport systems. The senior team has reported that the training was highly impactful. As a result, we intend to extend this initiative across the Department to ensure that DfI officials who are responsible for policy proposals are fully informed on the proposals that they put forward.I remain committed to working towards a more accessible and equitable transport network for all users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the development of the Desertcreat site as part of the Mid South West Growth Deal.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether funding was allocated to divisional offices to repair road signs damaged during Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: The initial intensive recovery phase following Storm Eowyn has been completed.	I would like to take this opportunity to thank all of the  staff who worked tirelessly to complete this work and reopen the road network as soon as safely possible.	However, Storm Eowyn has caused significant damage to our road infrastructure and some of the repair work is complex and time consuming. While my Department continues to work hard to complete this work, as well as continue to deliver its normal service, it is likely that it will take many months to have all the damage caused fully repaired.Where repairs are required to road signs or the associated supports, this is funded out of my Department’s resource budget, and I have made £2.8m of additional funding available to deal with a range of pressures on the resource budget. Where the damage to road signs or the associated supports is more extensive and requires the replacement of the infrastructure, this is funded out of my Department’s capital budget.  While some capital works associated with Storm Éowyn have been undertaken, this has been achieved by my Department reprioritising workloads within existing budgets. I will continue to press for additional capital funding to address the range of issues associated with Storm Éowyn including the necessary replacement of signage infrastructure.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the delivery of the Enhanced Investment Zone.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the rationale behind Education Authority transport provision being withdrawn from nursery providers.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the Home to School Transport policy and the Education Authority (EA) for the operation and delivery of school transport. I understand your query relates to the private hire of buses for educational trips rather than home to school transport. This is a non-statutory service provided for schools subject to availability and is entirely within the EA’s operational remit. I understand that the EA had paused the hire of its buses for nursery schools to allow it to investigate queries raised about child seats for younger children. The issue has been clarified, the pause lifted and nursery schools may apply to hire EA buses for school trips.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to provide an update on the baby loss certificate scheme.
answer_text: I am keen to see this initiative continue to be taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss. I want all parents and families who have suffered the devastating loss of a baby to feel that their grief is recognised. Primary legislation has been drafted which will be the start of the process to establish a scheme here. Approval to introduce the Bill was agreed in the Executive Committee meeting on 6th March so I hope to introduce the Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill into the Assembly in the next few weeks and when the Bill is enacted my Department can make regulations to establish a baby loss scheme. We will continue to work with the Department of Health (DoH) to specify the detail of this important initiative, to make sure it meets the needs and expectations of the bereaved families, and to scope delivery options. My officials continue to progress this initiative at pace. A more detailed schedule will be made available when the legislation has been considered by the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline how his Department is working with the Department of Health to ensure that all necessary provisions are in place to meet the needs of children with special educational needs.
answer_text: The provision of support from Allied Health Professionals is a matter for the Department of Health. In recognition of the health professionals support to meet the needs of children with special educational needs, my SEN Reform Agenda and Delivery Action Plan 2025-2030, includes a number of actions including investing in Healthcare Professionals to ensure that children and young people receive the right support, from the right people at the right time and in the right place.   Officials are working with colleagues in Health to mutually agree the best approach. This will require additional funding.The Public Health Agency in partnership with the Education Authority is currently undertaking a Review of health needs facing children attending special schools. This assessment aims to identify the specific health challenges faced by this population, evaluate the complexity of their needs, and ascertain the support systems necessary to ensure timely, child-specific care within the school environment. The final report is expected by the Minister of Health by the end of March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline when the consultation on action plans for hospital reconfiguration will begin with staff and members of the public.
answer_text: I launched a consultation on Hospitals – Creating a Network for Better Outcomes on 2 October last year, which closed on Friday 28 February this year. This paper set out a direction and strategic principles for collaboration to maximise our hospital resource and to ensure equity of access with better outcomes for all. Included in the document were a number of proposed actions to support delivery of this objective. As such, consultation with the public, including HSC staff, on action plans for hospital reconfiguration has already taken place.However, in relation to specific service changes, which were not a part of this consultation, as I have previously said a number of times, these will remain subject to the existing guidance on Change or Withdrawal of Services. This guidance includes public engagement and consultation.The Hospitals Network paper is underpinned by a number of existing service reviews and strategies which were clinician led and developed in partnership with those who use the services. To give some concrete examples, consultations have been carried out on stroke and breast assessment services, and a consultation on neurology services is expected later this year. There have also been local consultations undertaken by Trusts such as on the transformation of acute maternity services in the Northern Trust, following which all hospital births have now been consolidated on one site at Antrim Area Hospital.Ultimately, any changes to our hospitals will be led by the policy or operational owners at either Regional or Trust level, depending on what is most appropriate, and will include detailed financial investment requirements, projected productivity gains and, if applicable, efficiency savings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the operation of Section 26 of the Domestic Abuse and Civil Proceedings Act (Northern Ireland) 2021 in relation to information sharing with schools.
To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the independent review into the case of Jonathan Creswell.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline any steps he will take to ensure that the ring-fenced agricultural budget, agreed by the Executive, prioritises Direct Payments.
answer_text: Direct Payments form a significant part of the total income from farming in Northern Ireland.  On average around two thirds of farm incomes over the past five years have been made up of these payments.  I am delighted that the Executive formally recognised the importance of supporting farmers and earmarked this budget for 2025-2026 and future years.    This is a major win for the local farming community and underscores the critical importance of agriculture to Northern Ireland. I want to move away from old terminology such as “Direct Payments”.  Just last week I announced that the new programme of farm support will be referred to as the ‘Sustainable Agriculture Programme’ to relay clearly its importance in supporting change for a sustainable future.  It seeks to implement policies and strategies that benefit the environment and support our economically significant agri-food sector.  This past number of years have seen a significant amount of time and effort go into the co-design of this programme with all the key agriculture and environmental stakeholders. This process has brought a robustness and buy-in to the development process and has given me assurance that this programme will provide the right support for the industry and demonstrate good value for money for taxpayers. Working in partnership with our stakeholders, our focus is now on prioritising delivery and getting all of the schemes and measures up and running.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the proposed footpath on Mountjoy Road, Brocagh, Co. Tyrone.
answer_text: I recognise the need to provide attractive sustainable transport alternatives such as public transport, walking, wheeling, and cycling.  Without these, our people, our cities and our towns will choke.  People deserve better; a cleaner greener urban environment in which they can thrive. As a key element of the Climate Change Act, and in its own right, Active Travel will play a crucial role empowering people to make more sustainable choices, particularly for shorter everyday journeys. With safe, reliable, and accessible active travel infrastructure, individuals are more likely to embrace healthier alternatives to driving.My department has prepared a preliminary design for a new footway extending from St Brigid’s Primary School to the entrance of the Castlebay Centre on the Mountjoy Road.The proposed scheme includes some road realignment to improve pedestrian and road user safety on the Mountjoy Road.Land is required to facilitate construction of the scheme and my officials have commenced negotiations with the effected landowners.  However, at this early stage it is not yet possible to provide a time frame for delivery of this project.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered implementing specific Ambulance Service arrangements tailored for individuals living with dementia to ensure they are not required to go through Emergency Departments, which can often be overwhelming and unsuitable for their unique needs.
answer_text: The ambulance service continually works with all geographical HSC Trusts to continually improve access to relevant and suitable pathways for ambulance clinicians to refer into or convey directly to non-ED destinations. Presently, there is no exclusive pathway for people living with dementia as described here. However, there are a range of pathways where patients living with dementia can be referred to by ambulance clinicians, depending on their presenting clinical need. These would consist of, for example, Hospital at Home teams, Community Respiratory Teams, and Direct Assessment Units. These would ensure, where suitable, patients with dementia would avoid ED where the pathways are open, available, have space, and patients are clinically suitable.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the 2024-2025 pay award for teachers.

To ask the Minister of Health whether provisions are in place to ensure that staff employed by private providers contracted to deliver services on behalf of a Health and Social Care Trust receive equivalent pay adjustments to those awarded to directly employed health service staff.
answer_text: The pay awards that have been announced for hospital employed doctors and dentists and for staff employed under Agenda for Change terms and conditions, will be paid solely to directly employed HSC staff, for whom the Department has responsibility for rates of pay.While private sector providers may deliver services on behalf of the HSC, a contribution which is much appreciated and valued, they will do so within a pricing structure agreed with the relevant Trust.It is therefore for a matter for each private sector employer to consider and determine the rates of pay for its staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the total payments received from insurance companies in relation to inpatient treatments given to insured individuals injured as a result of a road traffic accident in each year since 2010, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) the systems that have been set up in Health and Social Care Trusts to ensure the request and collection of these payments whenever they are due.
answer_text: Information on total payments received from insurance companies from 2017/18 to 2023/24, listed by Health and Social Care Trust, is provided in Table 1 below. Information prior to 2017 is not available. Table 1YearBelfastHSC Trust£NorthernHSC Trust£SouthernHSC Trust£South EasternHSC Trust£WesternHSC Trust£TOTAL£2017/18954,805136,220242,740264,730203,6741,802,1692018/19997,886138,744213,430150,929261,7731,762,7632019/201,080,299148,801218,098214,665247,2461,909,1092020/21762,57290,154228,102176,643227,7991,485,2702021/221,434,225146,824168,396154,940261,8662,166,2512022/231,508,77968,079226,739167,673281,7112,252,9802023/241,336,69859,799263,999234,680281,6792,176,855Within Northern Ireland the debt recovery process is managed by colleagues in the Department for Communities (DfC) using the Social Security Agency’s Compensation Recovery Unit (CRU). DoH has limited input to the process apart from providing financial support to the administration costs of the scheme and making the enabling legislation which sets the maximum tariffs applicable in respect of injuries each financial year.The systems prevalent in the administration, operation and management of the Debt Recovery process are managed by DfC’s Corporate Debt Control Unit. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider implementing a flood alleviation scheme for Kings Row, Coalisland, to address recent flooding in the area.
answer_text: Following a period of very heavy rainfall associated with Storm Bert, the river drainage infrastructure on the Mousestown Stream, Coalisland became overwhelmed. The open watercourse enters a culvert via an inlet screen near the junction of Annagh Road and Stewartstown Road. The river surcharged at the mouth of this culvert and it overflowed. I am advised that this overflowing of the Mousetown Drain contributed to the flooding suffered by residents along Kings Row.My Department was proactive across the weekend, responding to over 600 calls for assistance across a wide geographical area. Preventative screen maintenance was undertaken across the North in the days prior to this event to help alleviate the impacts of flooding.  This preventative work contributed to hundreds of homes being protected.  Unfortunately, a number of residents across the North experienced flooding inside their homes including those at Kings Row, Coalisland. I can confirm that I have instructed officials to review my Department's response to this event, and the feasibility of any viable flood alleviation measures at this location will also be reviewed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has considered the forfeiture of an individual's Health and Social Care Pension, either partially or entirely, for those who have caused significant costs to the public purse by attempting to obstruct inquests, public inquiries or professional regulatory investigations.
answer_text: The HSC Pension Scheme regulations enable the Department to direct all or part of a member’s pension rights be forfeited if a member is convicted of specified offences, provided they were committed before the benefits become payable. These offences include an offence in connection with employment which has been certified by the Secretary of State either to have been gravely injurious to the State or liable to lead to serious loss of confidence in the public service; an offence of treason or an offence under the Official Secrets Acts for which the member was sentenced to a term of imprisonment of at least 10 years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any advice he has received from the Chief Medical Officer (CMO) in relation to matters around the Duty of Candour; (ii) any specific recommendations the CMO has made to him; and (iii) whether the CMO addressed concerns about potential legal or disciplinary consequences related to the Duty of Candour.
answer_text: I can confirm that the Chief Medical Officer has not provided any advice to me on this policy matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given his recent allocation of up to £13 million to enhance services for children with disabilities, what measures will be put in place to ensure that families are positively impacted by this funding.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current average waiting times for individuals seeking fertility treatment, specifically (i) the waiting time from GP referral to the first appointment with a consultant; and (ii) the waiting time from the initial consultation with a fertility specialist to the commencement of treatment.
answer_text: Fertility services within local Health and Social Care Trusts are provided as part of the gynaecology service for women rather than commissioned as a separate service.  Provision of the service is heavily reliant on clinicians who have a particular interest in this field. Each Trust has been provided with funding for fertility nurse advisor input as an integral part of supporting fertility investigations at a local Trust level.   Table 1 below outlines the current average waiting times for individuals seeking fertility treatment, specifically in response to (i) the waiting time from GP referral to the first appointment with a consultant across Belfast, South Eastern, Southern and Western HSC Trusts. Baseline investigations are carried out and advice is offered to women within gynaecology services with referral to the Regional Fertility Clinic in Belfast Health and Social Care Trust for consultation with a fertility specialist if subfertility is over 1 year or on completion of investigations.In response to your request for detail of the current average waiting times for individuals seeking fertility treatment, specifically for (ii) the waiting time from the initial consultation with a fertility specialist to the commencement of treatment, I wish to highlight that the waiting time from initial consultation to the commencement of treatment can vary for each individual, depending on the patients’ person-centred medical need and any investigations required to make an intention to treat decision.In terms of local HSC Trust provision, patients accessing local fertility services are assessed on a case by case basis, the next stage in the treatment pathway is dependent on multiple factors such as whether or not there is a need for surgical intervention/investigations, onward referral to the Regional Fertility Centre in Belfast HSC Trust, or if the patient is deemed suitable for referral on to the local nurse advisor to commence ovulation induction treatment. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether maintenance antibiotics are offered as part of the treatment regimen for patients with Parkinson’s disease.
answer_text: Antibiotics are offered, when clinically indicated, to people with Parkinson's disease who have infections.Parkinson’s disease is not an infectious disease, and there is no NICE guidance on Parkinson's disease that recommends the use of antibiotics in its management, therefore maintenance antibiotics are not offered as part of its treatment regimen.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the next steps in the process of establishing a statutory Inquiry and a financial Redress Scheme for those affected by Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.

To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the provision of diagnostic services for adolescent patients aged 12 to 17 years old with (i) attention deficit hyperactivity disorder; and (ii) attention deficit disorder.
answer_text: ADHD is the preferred diagnostic name as ADD is no longer used by name as a diagnosis. Based on the presentation of ADHD symptoms, there are three types of the disorder—namely inattentive type, hyperactive/impulsive type, and combined type.Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis differs across Trusts as they have created services in response to both population demand and available expertise within their respective Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there are currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand is leading to individuals waiting longer for assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.Four of the five Trusts provide assessments to under 18’s from the age of 6 years old. One Trust focuses children’s assessments on the under 12’s and does not currently offer assessments to the 12-18 age group. Officials have met with all Trusts individually to map current paediatric ADHD and ASD referral, assessment and intervention pathways. Following this mapping exercise, Department officials are working with colleagues from the Public Health Agency to develop a regionally consistent and defined neurodevelopmental pathway that complies with NICE guidelines. This work will be overseen by the Joint Children’s Commissioning Group chaired by two Deputy Directors one from my Department and one from the PHA.The Department of Health is committed to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based pathways for ADHD diagnosis and support. In line with the usual process for commissioning services, any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the next steps in the process of establishing a statutory Inquiry and a financial Redress Scheme for those affected by Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses.
answer_text: We thank all those who took time to respond and who attended one of the 16 public events.  In total 269 responses were received.  We are committed to publishing an analysis of consultation responses, which we hope to do in the coming weeks.We need time to consider them in full, but there are many areas of broad agreement, or where positive amendments can be made. However, there remain many sensitive policy issues which will require discussion with Executive Colleagues before any decision can be taken. We aim to do this work by the end of 2024. Once the draft bill is agreed by the Executive, it will be introduced into the Assembly for it be considered fully. In parallel, we are continuing to establish the practical requirements for the inquiry and redress service.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he has plans to update the criteria for awarding intimidation points, through the Housing Executive, to victims of domestic abuse and violence.

To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the decline in the achievement of the 62-day and 14-day waiting time targets for breast cancer, with both targets decreasing from Q1 2023 to Q1 2024; (ii) to detail the factors that have contributed to this decline; (iii) how his Department plans to address these issues to ensure timely diagnosis; and (iv) ,given preparation for any additional strain during Breast Cancer Awareness Month in October, and the upcoming winter season, to detail the (a) immediate; and (b) long-term measures that will be implemented to improve the achievement of
answer_text: The decline in achievement of the 62-day and 14-day waiting time targets for breast cancer is unacceptable but is symptomatic of the pressures facing all cancer services. Increasing demand, staffing pressures, an aging demography and other factors mean that performance against these targets has fallen to unacceptably low levels. On 25 September 2024, I announced plans to establish a regional Breast Cancer Service, with a regional waiting list for breast assessment to be established in the coming months. A wider review of breast cancer services will be conducted at pace and seeks to ensure that the regional breast cancer service can deliver timely access to breast cancer diagnosis and care.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline how many businesses have benefited from the Back in Business rates support scheme within the last financial year.
answer_text: In the financial year 2023/24, 99 businesses benefitted from the Back in Business rate support scheme.The Back in Business rate support scheme has been well received since it was reintroduced and opened for applications in May 2024. A further 18 businesses have received a 50% reduction in business rates for up to two years providing almost £41,000 in rates support. I was really pleased to visit Coleraine last month and meet two businesses who have received support from the scheme. One of these businesses occupied a building which had been vacant for 10 years right in the heart of the town. It was really encouraging to hear how the scheme is supporting new businesses while giving premises which had been vacant for a long time a new lease of life.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any issues his Department had in utilising early years data to forecast the number of children with special educational needs (SEN) to inform allied health professionals and nursing resourcing decisions; (ii) how his Department collaborate with the Department of Education to support children with SEN once they have been identified; and (iii) any specific gaps in service delivery for children with SEN his Department is aiming to address through its strategic plans and policies.
answer_text: The Department recognises that early years data can be useful to forecast the number of children with special educational needs (SEN) and inform workforce planning. It should be noted that developmental delay does not inevitably mean that a child will have SEN upon entry to school. The Department of Health (DoH), Education (DE) and the Public Health Agency (PHA) have commenced a regional scoping exercise of antenatal and early years data to develop a model to better forecast the number of children with SEN. It has identified several robust datasets that potentially have a causal link to developmental delay and SEN. It has also identified data quality and completeness issues, which are currently being assessed.At an individual level, data flows between Trusts and the Education Authority during the SEN statementing process to assess a child’s therapeutic, medical and educational needs. This is used to inform resource planning at a local level. At a Departmental level, Health and Education collaborate across several policy and operational issues, largely through the End-to-End Review of SEN and strategic discussions on placements, statementing, provision of therapies, and workforce planning. In addition, Education have shared models and projections on SEN with my officials to inform planning and decisions for Allied Health Profressionals.The number of children with SEN has risen sharply in recent years, which has exceeded the capacity of the HSC workforce. Therefore, Trusts have adopted a model of clinical prioritisation to ensure that those with the highest level of therapeutic needs are supported to access the curriculum. In 2022, my Department committed recurrent funding of an additional £2.5 million to addressing the net increase of children and young people requiring Special School placements. Work is currently underway to assess the impact of this intervention and inform future decisions on HSC provision for children with SEN.It is recognised that the current model of AHP provision within Special Schools requires a focused renewal to improve integration with Education and develop a greater portfolio of universal supports and improve standardisation. PHA is leading on work with key partners to develop and implement a standardised tiered model of AHP support across NI.The Departments of Health and Education also recognise the need to engage with parents/carers, schools and their surrounding communities in a meaningful way to enhance the supports around the children and young people that meet their needs and manage these comprehensively.  To support this, a jointly funded Partnership Lead post was appointed in 2022 to develop enhanced partnerships and develop innovative ways to capture the voice of the children and young people within the Special School environment as an initial focus for this work.
