question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) what additional resources will be allocated to Allied Health Professional services for children with special educational needs in this fiscal year, including any specific programs or initiatives that have received funding; and (ii) the strategic plans or policies aimed at improving service delivery and addressing current gaps.
answer_text: (i) My department having recognised the increased demand and complexity of need of Children and Young People with Special Education Needs (SEN), and to support this, have made investments into Allied Health Professionals (AHP) services. At this stage, in light of the very challenging financial situation - there is no specific further additional funding identified / available for allocation for the 2024/25 financial year for Allied Health Professional Services for children with SEN. However, my Department has made significant investment in recent years in recognition of the increased pressures in this area, most recently with an additional £2.5 million being invested in services in 2022/23-2023/24. My department continues to work closely with Department of Education (DE) on SEN placements and the demand for AHP supports in the school environment. Work is underway to better use Early Years data to more accurately forecast the number of children with SEN and inform decisions on AHP and Nursing resourcing.(ii) The growth in the SEN population and changing profile is mirrored in the continuum of HSC services for children and families, specifically those with learning disabilities and Autism.  Demand continues to outstrip the supply of services provided by Health and Education; therefore, we need to deliver services differently.The Department is finalising a Framework for Children with Disabilities, which seeks to expand the level and diversity of supports in the community through the lens of early-stage intervention. In addition, work is underway to better support the therapeutic and healthcare needs of children with disabilities, as well as our collaboration with Education to support children with Special Educational Needs.The Department is fully supportive of the Department of Education SEN End to End Review as the key vehicle for change across government. Officials have been fully engaged in this work alongside several other DE initiatives to improve outcomes for children with SEN. It is important that opportunities are maximised to align the SEN review to ongoing HSC Reform. There is an urgent need to get ahead of the changes in population and complexity of need to ensure that sufficient and adequate services are available. Work is underway to better use data collected by HSC. A scoping exercise of Trust data has recently been carried out and further work is ongoing to allow predictions of arising need, and to enable appropriate long-term planning with regard to placements and workforce planning.  This is a key priority for both Department of Health and Department for Education.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline his Department's investment in repairing and maintaining footpaths.
answer_text: As Minister, I am very aware of the need for investment to address the repair and maintenance of, in particular, older flagged footways across the North, which has resulted from years of underfunding and austerity by the British Government.The scope of the works necessary to address this problem will require investment over a number of years.My Department’s opening budget allocation for road maintenance for the 2024-25 financial year has now been finalised and my officials are currently developing the detailed budget breakdowns. This will include investment for the upgrading of existing footways. This is addition to the footways being upgraded as part of the carriageway resurfacing programme to be undertaken during the current financial year.Work on developing the resurfacing programme is currently being completed. Upon completion, details of all schemes, including those for footways, will be included in the forthcoming reports presented to Councils prior to general release to the public.The costs for repairing footways by patching are included in the overall patching allocation and are still to be developed at the lower level.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for his assessment of whether NIEA require additional powers to ensure action can be taken against polluters of Lough Neagh.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the progress of implementing minimum unit pricing for alcohol, following the consultation in 2022.
To ask the Minister of Health to outline what financial support is provided to childcare providers that look after children with additional needs.
answer_text: The Department of Health has, since 2016, made available   recurrent resources to support the inclusion of children with additional needs in childcare settings through the annual Fair Play grant scheme. This scheme is administered by the Childcare Partnership which is hosted by the Department of Health. Applications are invited from the pre-school, after-school, creche and day care sectors. The funding provides support measures with a focus on the individual needs of children in registered community, voluntary and private childcare throughout the academic year from September to June. An example of support measures would be additional staff capacity. In the 2023/24 financial year £395k was available in this scheme. Funds were issued to 59 childcare settings supporting the inclusion of 242 children with a wide range of needs. The scheme encourages a partnership approach between childcare providers and parents to ensure any beneficial practice in the childcare setting can be translated into the home setting. Examples of this would be sensory play techniques, the use of Makaton and a range of communication strategies. In the advent of additional resources this scheme, would be expanded to include further support to children and childcare providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has received correspondence from the Policing Board requesting that Injury on Duty and Ill Health Retirement are removed from its responsibility.
answer_text: The Chair of the Policing Board has written to me on the on 17 April to request a continued focus on giving effect to the recommendations of the 2020 NIAO report relating to the Police Injury on Duty Scheme. The Chair has further asked that the roles of the Board, Police Service of Northern Ireland and the Department are considered in relation to both Ill Health Retirement and Injury on Duty awards.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on work to progress the recommendations of the Truth Recovery Design Panel's report into Mother and Baby Institutions, Magdalene Laundries and Workhouses in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Significant progress has been made on the implementation of the five key recommendations including establishing a victim-survivor Consultation Forum, which has met on 29 occasions. A non-statutory 10-person Independent Panel was appointed in April 2023 to take forward the first part of the investigation, and PRONI has commenced cataloguing and digitising 4,500 institutional records.In addition, a specialist and dedicated support service has assisted approximately 300 people to date, which is a critical element to support victims and survivors.A key upcoming milestone will be the public consultation on the proposed public inquiry and redress schemes’ legislation, which will need to be considered by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether funding is available to commence the new build for Gaelscoil Uí Néill, Coalisland.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the recent tragic loss of life on the road, how he will progress the delivery of the A5 upgrade as a priority.
answer_text: Firstly, I wish to send my heartfelt condolences to the family and friends of Caolan Devlin who tragically lost his life in a road traffic collision on the A5 on the 5 March.   Since coming to office, I have put the progression of the A5 at the top of my list of priorities and I am determined to do all that I can to progress this crucial project to save lives.  My Department received the final advisory Report from the Planning Appeals Commission (PAC) on 31 October 2023 in relation to the latest public inquiry on the project. I have been fully briefed on current progress and on the advisory recommendations of the PAC. Careful consideration is being given to the points raised and detailed recommendations made within this report. Once my officials have completed this work and presented all relevant information for my consideration, I intend to make an announcement on the project. When I make my announcement, I will do so in accordance with the relevant statutory procedures and on the basis of all of the evidence, representations, and advice which I have received from my officials, statutory agencies North and South, members of the public and from all other bodies which have participated in the process to date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline what plans she has to introduce legislation to remove police pensions and injury benefits from the Policing Board's responsibility.

To ask the Minister of Health, further to the temporary reinstatement of the Cross-border Healthcare Directive in June 2021, (i) whether there will be a permanent replacement for the Directive; and (ii) to outline the rationale for this decision.
To ask the Minister of Education what work is being undertaken to address the problems with the new NI Supply Teacher Register system.
answer_text: The Department funds the Education Authority to administer the NI Supply Teacher Register (NISTR) which is effectively a booking system enabling schools to source and engage professionally qualified teachers who have undergone a range of pre-employment checks.  On the third working day of each month, a file of approved (signed-off) bookings is extracted from the NISTR and sent to the Department for processing of salary to temporary teachers on schools’ behalf.There have been a number of issues regarding the implementation of a new NISTR system and the timely payment of temporary teachers. The Department and the EA continue to work together intensively, alongside stakeholder groups, to ensure that immediate steps are taken to ensure staff are paid for their valuable service.  In the current exceptional circumstances, particularly in light of the cost-of-living crisis, the Department has asked the EA to process interim emergency payments to all teachers impacted as a matter of urgency.Due to administrative errors in extracting the file and uploading it to the payroll system; 309 temporary and supply teachers received only partial salary on 18 October for bookings that were approved by schools and should have been paid under normal circumstances.  The EA has sincerely apologised for this error and is processing interim emergency payments to reach these teachers by 25 October.The other factor that has caused problems in the timely payment of temporary teachers relates to the fact that the appropriate approval processes at school level were not completed in line with the payroll deadline.  This prevented payment being made on the planned date for an estimated 300 teachers.  Incorrect data input in respect of hours worked also appears to have featured in the sign-off process.  The EA is contacting all relevant schools to seek confirmation of attendance, check period of service and obtain other necessary sign-off information to enable further interim emergency payments to pursued.  In respect of the system issues, a number of fixes have been applied to address identified bugs and provide additional functionality for example, in line with strong school user feedback, a search by teacher name or TR number was added on 22 September.  Work continues to make further improvements and changes to system logic based on user feedback.  Planned additional features will continue to be added throughout the developmental phase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will reinstate the Cross Border Directive scheme.
To ask the Minister of Health how he will reduce waiting times for elective surgeries in the absence of the Cross Border Directive scheme.
answer_text: I continue to do the best I can to deliver high quality Health and Social Care services for the public with the resources I have available.In June 2021, I published the Elective Care Framework which sets out a five-year plan, with a dual focus on clearing the existing backlog of patients waiting for assessment and treatment across Northern Ireland and closing the gap between capacity and demand.The Framework sets out a detailed roadmap for addressing hospital waiting lists. It covers 55 actions to reduce our waiting lists and to improve capacity across the elective care system. I have asked my officials to create an Elective Care Management Team (ECMT) to oversee implementation of the Framework. The team brings together clinicians and senior managers from across health and social care system and the Department to lead a strategic, whole system, integrated approach to the delivery of elective care as articulated in the Elective Care Framework. There are a number of initiatives in the Framework that are already well underway:The introduction of mega clinics to maximise patient throughput. The introduction of mega clinics to maximise patient throughput.  Overall, as of 11 October 2022, 10,700 patients have been treated, with 4,500 treated so far, this financial year. The Day Procedure Centre (DPC) at Lagan Valley Hospital (LVH) has been operational since Autumn 2020 and supports a range of specialties from across the region to undertake the most urgent clinical cases. Approximately 4,600 patients have been treated at the Regional Day Procedure Centre at LVH, and approximately 9,000 patients have received endoscopy and Cysto/TP biopsy procedures at the centre. This is additional regional capacity to help reduce long waits.In 22/23 activity will increase, with Lagan Valley Hospital DPC intending to deliver over 900 theatre lists per year with over 5,000 patients treated across ENT, urology, general surgery, and gynaecology alongside 20 regional endoscopy lists which will see an extra 6,000 patients per year.Omagh Hospital has been announced as the next Regional Day Procedure Centre and has been provided with additional funding to enhance capacity for Elective Care Services.  It is anticipated that the Omagh DPC will be fully operational in early 2023.Omagh Hospital Day Procedure Centre will see seven regional theatre lists per week across urology and general surgery – two specialities with some of the longest waits across the HSC. This means an extra 1,750 patients across these specialities will be treated per year when fully implemented.Furthermore, 10 regional endoscopy sessions at Omagh Hospital will see an extra 3,000 patients per year.In addition, the Duke of Connaught Orthopaedic Day Procedure Unit at Musgrave Park has been operational since April 2022. question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) what action she is taking to address the delay in processing applications for Free School Meals and Uniform Grants; (ii) whether payments for free school meals will be backdated; and (iii) to outline the rationale for this position.
answer_text: In recognising the importance of pupils’ Free School Meal and Uniform Grant entitlement being confirmed before the start of the academic year, the EA has undertaken extensive communications explaining to families that in order to ensure they were able access these supports from the start of the academic year, they should apply before 31 July. This was communicated via social media, the EA website and through letters issued to applicants who received support last year encouraging them to apply again for the current school year.  While many parents did apply early enough to ensure entitlement was in place from the start of the academic year, unfortunately this was not the case for all applications. The EA continues to make every effort to process applications as quickly as possible and has consistently met its target of processing 95% of free school meals and uniform grant applications within four weeks. As at 14 October, the EA was processing applications received on 16th September.In order to support parents in their applications, the EA provides extensive guidance materials and technical support. When an application is submitted without the appropriate documentation, all such applicants are sent a tailored email response explaining the correct documentation that they are required to provide. It is necessary to verify applicants’ eligibility in order to ensure that public funds are used correctly.Free school meals are a benefit in kind and there is therefore no facility available for providing backdated payments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department has taken to restore full daycare and respite provision for people with disabilities and learning difficulties in the (i) Northern Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) Southern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Remobilisation of adult daycare and respite is coordinated at a regional level to ensure consistency across all 5 Trusts. Adult day care is currently below pre-pandemic capacity, therefore I have asked my officials to provide regular updates on service provision levels. Data on day care, short breaks and transport is provided on the Department’s website and will be updated on a monthly basis. https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/articles/remobilisation-learning-disability-servicesTo improve the level of provision my officials have worked with the Public Health Agency to develop a Pathway for remobilisation and issued updated public health guidance (also available at the above link), which will enable Trusts to better balance Covid-19 considerations with wider risks to the mental health and wellbeing of service users, families and carers. My officials have agreed to meet a joint All Party Group on Carers and Learning Disability on 14 September 2022, to provide a further update on remobilisation and the work undertaken by the Department to manage Covid-19 risk.	It is regrettable that services have yet to be fully restored to pre-pandemic capacity and I sincerely apologise to those families that continue to receive a partial service.
